title: Transformer Model Families
description: Families built from repeated attention and feed-forward blocks, commonly used for language, code, and many multimodal systems.
openingSummary: Transformer families are the broadest branch in the atlas because they separate the reusable block recipe from any one named checkpoint: stacked attention and feed-forward layers update token representations in parallel, then a decoding or readout head turns those hidden states into the next action, label, or prediction.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: A transformer family page is about the shared architecture recipe, not one checkpoint. The common pattern is tokenization, embeddings, repeated attention plus feed-forward blocks, and a head that turns hidden states into an output. Decoder-only stacks lean on causal attention for next-token prediction, while other transformer branches reuse the same block idea for bidirectional reading, cross-modal fusion, or encoder-decoder behavior.

howItDiffers.title: How It Differs From Nearby Families
howItDiffers.body: Compared with a diffusion model, a transformer usually reasons over a structured sequence rather than a denoising trajectory. Compared with a multimodal model page, this family description is about the block recipe first and the input mix second. Compared with an omni model family, transformers do not automatically imply unified turn-taking across text, image, audio, and tools; many omni systems still use transformer internals under a broader product loop.

representativePatterns.title: Representative Patterns
representativePatterns.body: When readers say GPT-style, encoder-only, seq2seq, retrieval-aware, or long-context, they are usually naming a transformer branch or operating pattern rather than leaving the transformer family entirely. Attention, multi-head attention, multi-query attention, and grouped-query attention matter here because they change memory use, latency, or how much context the same stack can afford.
